allocations.rationale: Sharpe 1.22 | Alpha +18.62% — Top-quintile risk-adjusted return; beta 1.11 (takes full market upside); 3y CAGR 26.9%. Genuine active alpha, not fee drag.
Sharpe 1.00 | Alpha +14.48% — Lower volatility (β=0.89) than mid-cap category avg, yet 5y CAGR 21.8%. Largest AUM (₹243Cr) = stability.
Sharpe 1.36 | Alpha +23.22% — Highest Sharpe in mid-cap universe. 3y CAGR 27.8%. Information ratio 2.58 (genuine stock-picking edge). Beta 0.90 = lower drawdown.
Sharpe 0.90 | Alpha +10.78% — 5y CAGR 22.2%, β=1.05 (appropriate for small cap), TE 10.5% (active management, not closet indexing). Solid alpha in small-cap segment.
Sharpe 2.65 | ER 0.20% — Exceptional Sharpe ratio (market-leading). International diversification (USD exposure, ₹ hedge). 1y return 34.2%, 3y 23.8%. Beta 0.07 (uncorrelated to Nifty).
Core stability. 5y CAGR 15.4%, Sharpe 0.43 (lower risk volatility σ=13.7%). Acts as portfolio ballast against equity drawdowns. Largest AUM (₹248Cr).
Sharpe 0.78 | Alpha +7.24% — 5y CAGR 21.3%, balanced exposure across cap sizes. Lower tracking error (6.4%) suggests disciplined implementation.
Tax efficiency: ₹1.5L Section 80C deduction (saves ₹45k tax at 30% slab annually). Sharpe 0.46, 3y CAGR 21.0%. Post-3y lock-in, gains are equity-taxed at 12.5% (vs 30% for debt).

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open Direct Mutual Fund Accounts on Kuvera, Groww, or MFU (direct plans, no distributor fees). Link PAN and bank account. Step 2: Deploy initial ₹40L lumpsum within 1 week across 8 funds per allocation table (₹8L to Motilal L&M, ₹6L to HDFC Mid Cap, ₹6L to WhiteOak Mid Cap, ₹6L to Invesco Smallcap, ₹4L to S&P 500 Index, ₹4L to ICICI Large Cap, ₹4L to Nippon Multi Cap, ₹2L to WhiteOak ELSS). Step 3: Set up monthly SIPs (₹1.5L total) automating into each fund per allocation percentages (₹30k to Motilal L&M, ₹22.5k each to HDFC Mid Cap / WhiteOak Mid Cap / Invesco Smallcap, ₹15k each to S&P 500 / ICICI Large Cap / Nippon Multi Cap, ₹7.5k to WhiteOak ELSS). Step 4: Tax-optimize ELSS by investing ₹1.5L annually in January each year to maximize Section 80C deduction (saves ₹45k tax at 30% slab annually). Lock-in 3 years; post-unlock gains are equity-taxed at 12.5% on amounts >₹1.25L.
review_checkpoints: Quarterly (every 3 months) review: Monitor each fund's 3m, 6m, 1y returns vs. category median; exit if underperforms peer median for 2+ consecutive quarters or if manager changes / style drift detected
Sharpe Ratio Degradation: Exit if Sharpe drops >0.2 points while category Sharpe remains stable; signals loss of risk-adjusted edge
Tracking Error Explosion: Flag if TE rises >30% above historical level (signals rogue position-taking); exit if unexplained
Portfolio Beta Drift: Exit and replace if beta <0.9 for 12+ months (suggests manager playing too conservatively, missing upside)
SIP Continuity: Reconcile bank statements monthly; fix any failed ₹1.5L monthly debits same day to maintain dollar-cost averaging discipline
Portfolio Rebalancing Drift: Rebalance quarterly if any position drifts >2 percentage points from target allocation; sell winners, buy losers to lock gains
Post-3-Year ELSS Unlock: After 3-year lock-in, exit WhiteOak ELSS and reallocate proceeds to equity fund with highest current Sharpe ratio to maintain active alpha generation
plan.rationale: This is a concentrated, conviction-driven portfolio of 8 funds selected on two equally-weighted criteria: (1) Sharpe ratio / risk-adjusted return and (2) alpha generation relative to category peer medians. Portfolio-weighted post-tax CAGR is 13.3% (after 30% tax slab, 12.5% LTCG on equity gains). Expected terminal value at 18-year horizon: ₹12.85Cr (₹85L above ₹12Cr goal). Allocation balances alpha-generating active managers (Motilal Oswal L&M: +18.62% alpha, HDFC Mid Cap: +14.48% alpha, WhiteOak Mid Cap: +23.22% alpha) with portfolio stability anchors (ICICI Large Cap: Sharpe 0.43, largest AUM stability) and international uncorrelated hedge (S&P 500 Index: Sharpe 2.65, β=0.07 to Nifty). 100% allocation to equity-taxed funds (≥65% equity exposure) to exploit 12.5% LTCG tax advantage vs. 30% slab-taxed debt instruments. Portfolio beta ≈0.92 provides full market participation (beta 1.11 in Motilal L&M) while reducing volatility vs. pure small-cap exposure. SIP-based rupee-cost averaging over ₹18L/year initial commitment ($1.5L/month) compounds wealth via market cycles. ELSS allocation provides ₹1.5L annual Section 80C deduction (saves ₹45k tax/year at 30% slab). Quantitative discipline: Quarterly monitoring of Sharpe, alpha, tracking error; exit thresholds include Sharpe degradation >0.2pp or 2 consecutive quarters of category underperformance. Rebalancing ±2pp drift maintains target risk profile. Suitable for high-net-worth, aggressive-appetite, long-horizon investor (18 years) seeking tax-efficient wealth accumulation.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning: This is a conviction-driven, genuinely ACTIVE portfolio. Passive instrument fraction is only 0.10 because only the Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index (10% allocation) is truly passive; all other 7 funds (90% of equity allocation) are actively managed with explicit stock-picking/sector mandates (Large & Midcap, Midcap, Smallcap, Multi Cap, ELSS categories). Turnover score of 0.35 reflects quarterly rebalancing checkpoints (not annual), performance-triggered exits (Sharpe degradation >0.2pp, 2 consecutive quarters underperformance, style drift), and tactical monitoring—more frequent than buy-and-hold but not constant trading. Cost emphasis is 0.25 because while the plan uses direct plans and emphasizes tax optimization, the PRIMARY selection criterion stated explicitly is 'Sharpe ratio / risk-adjusted return and alpha generation relative to category peer medians'—not cost minimization. Expense ratios are cited but subordinate to performance. Research vs cost is 0.15 (very research-heavy) because the plan emphasizes deep quantitative fund analysis: alpha (+18.62% to +23.22%), Sharpe ratios (0.43 to 2.65), information ratios (2.58), tracking error (10.5%), beta dynamics, manager stability (largest AUM), and individual fund conviction—the opposite of broad-market passive indexing. Time horizon alignment is 0.60 (moderate-to-long) because while the stated horizon is 18 years with SIP-based compounding, the implementation involves quarterly tactical reviews, dynamic exit thresholds, and performance-triggered rebalancing within that long horizon, rather than pure buy-and-hold passivity. Portfolio activeness score is 0.26 because the portfolio is weighted heavily toward genuinely active funds: Motilal L&M (20%, beta 1.11, alpha +18.62%), HDFC Mid Cap (15%, alpha +14.48%), WhiteOak Mid Cap (15%, alpha +23.22%, IR 2.58), and Invesco Smallcap (15%, alpha +10.78%, TE 10.5%)—all exhibiting high tracking error or information ratio indicative of genuine stock-picking conviction. Only 10% is allocated to a truly passive index fund. ICICI Large Cap (10%) shows lower Sharpe (0.43) and no alpha, suggesting closet-indexing or defensive positioning. Nippon Multi Cap (10%) and ELSS (5%) are moderately active. Weighted across the portfolio: ~65% genuinely active (low scores), 10% truly passive (high score), 25% moderately active (mid scores), yielding a portfolio-weighted activeness score of 0.26. Overall, this plan sits firmly on the ACTIVE end of the APS spectrum, reflecting sophisticated alpha-seeking behavior with research-driven fund selection, performance-based rebalancing discipline, and conviction in active management edge relative to market indices.

pqs.reasoning: STRENGTHS: (1) Goal alignment is strong—the 18-year horizon perfectly matches retirement at 52, projected terminal value of ₹12.85Cr exceeds the ₹12Cr goal, and the entire portfolio is explicitly optimized on Sharpe ratio (investor's stated preference). Allocation strategy (8-fund, conviction-driven, alpha-focused) aligns with investor's quantitative, data-driven mindset. (2) Diversification is solid—6 fund houses, balanced cap structure (30% midcap, 20% large & midcap, 15% smallcap, 10% large cap, 10% multi-cap), 10% international (uncorrelated USD hedge), all equity-taxed instruments. (3) Internal consistency: allocations sum to 100%, SIP amounts sum to ₹150k/month, lumpsum sums to ₹40L. Each fund has clear Sharpe/alpha rationale; review checkpoints quantified (Sharpe degradation >0.2pp, 2-quarter underperformance thresholds, beta drift <0.9 for 12m). (4) Tax efficiency is well-reasoned—ELSS correctly identified for ₹1.5L annual 80C deduction (saves ₹45k/year at 30% slab), all other funds are ≥65% equity to exploit 12.5% LTCG taxation vs. 30% slab-taxed debt. Plan explicitly avoids debt/liquid funds for a 30% slab investor. GAPS: (1) Risk-return: 'risks' array is completely empty despite investor's quantitative expectations. Plan lacks downside scenario analysis (e.g., 2008-style crash impact), no explicit maximum drawdown, no confidence intervals or sensitivity tables on projected 13.3% post-tax CAGR. (2) Diversification: 30% in midcap concentration (HDFC Mid Cap 15% + WhiteOak Mid Cap 15%) is material; no sector-level analysis provided. (3) Internal inconsistencies: S&P 500 Index fund listed with 'beta: 0.07' is suspect (US equity should have higher correlation/beta to rupee swings, not that low); 'rebalancing_guidelines' field is empty despite detailed checkpoint rules elsewhere. (4) Tax efficiency gaps: ELSS ₹1.5L annual investment is mentioned in Step 4 rationale but the SIP allocation shows only ₹7.5k/month (₹90k/year)—the plan does not clearly specify the additional ₹60k lumpsum topup needed to reach ₹1.5L limit. No explicit statement of 'old regime' assumption (plan assumes 80C applicability, correct for '30_percent_slab' but should be stated). (5) Goal coverage: retirement goal is detailed, but research institute/sabbatical funding goal is mentioned but not separately modeled; no post-52 portfolio management strategy discussed. (6) For a data-driven investor, projected returns should include pre-tax, post-tax breakdown by return component (capital appreciation vs. dividend) and holding-period assumptions. Overall: plan is disciplined, well-structured, and tax-aware, but lacks the probabilistic downside analysis and mathematical rigor a quantitative investor (especially one accustomed to research methodology) would expect.
